findings.description: Rules section references rules via absolute local filesystem paths that are machine-specific and will break on any other machine or CI environment.
Branch check is inline prose without a labeled '## Step 1 — Branch check' heading, unlike every other command.
Branch check is inline prose without a labeled '## Step 1 — Branch check' heading, unlike every other command.
No branch check step at all; every other write-capable command guards on main/master before doing any work.
No branch check step even though it writes files (.claude/rules/, CLAUDE.md) that should only be committed from main.
Uses free-form section headings instead of the '## Step N — Name' pattern used by all other commands.
Uses 'source': './' with no explicit include/files list; every file added to the repo root is silently bundled into the plugin distribution.
Bootstrap procedure instruction ('Perform this scan during project bootstrap') is mixed with standing operational rules, causing agents to potentially re-execute it on every load.
Mermaid flow diagram shows approval nodes with only |yes| branches; none have a |no|/stop path even though the command halts on decline.
Approval gate in Task 3 is buried mid-list rather than called out as a hard stop, risking agents treating it as optional prose.
Conditional self-advance ('proceed to Step 3 when approved') conflicts with the explicit stop-and-wait gate pattern used in Steps 4 and 5.
Jekyll exclude list manually enumerates directories; every new top-level directory must be manually added or it will be included in the site build.
No branch check step even though it commits in Step 5 Task 6, diverging from every other file-writing command.
Uses free-form section headings ('Assessment', 'Full', 'Gap') instead of '## Step N — Name' pattern.
Uses free-form section headings ('Assessment', 'Full', 'Gap') instead of '## Step N — Name' pattern.
The docs page clarifies 'the file is not procedural' but the shipped rules/safety.md has no equivalent — agents read the rule file, not the doc.
Rules index page is a stub with no child-file links, no summary of what each rule covers, and no guidance on when to use which.
No '## Stop conditions' section; uses '## Approval gates' instead, breaking the structural template every other command page follows.
Scoped verification pass is numbered '8.5' in the doc but '7.5' in the command definition, creating a numbering mismatch.
See also entries use ' - ' (hyphen) as separator; majority of other command docs use ' — ' (em-dash).
See also entries use ' - ' (hyphen) as separator; majority of other command docs use ' — ' (em-dash).
Task 6 uses git add -A, contradicting CLAUDE.md safety rule to prefer adding specific files by name.
No explicit 'wait for approval before Step 4' gate in closing instruction, unlike the uniform gate language in Steps 2, 4, and 5.
No 'wait for approval' closing gate statement analogous to Steps 2, 4, and 5, making the stopping behaviour implicit rather than enforced.
Writing style block (em-dash policy) is duplicated verbatim in legal.md, log.md, and manifest.md — four copies that will drift independently.
Reference-mode description says 'prints snippet to chat if CLAUDE.md does not exist' but command now prompts to create CLAUDE.md first (commit 3d87e3d).
Stop condition 'No CLAUDE.md + Reference mode: snippet printed, nothing written' is now only one branch, not the only outcome — stale after commit 3d87e3d.
Intro says 'two confirmation gates' but the command has three: risk warning (Step 2), plan confirmation (Step 4), and force-push confirmation (Step 7).
Docs page omits the 'Document versions instead of containerizing' guidance block (mobile, desktop, browser-extension, library cases) present in the step file.
Top-level description field duplicates the identical string already inside plugins[0].description on line 12.
Commands table links to docs/commands/*.md with no indication that actual command logic lives in commands/*.md — the distinction is invisible at point of discovery.
git filter-repo called preferred over filter-branch but the command block uses filter-branch — no filter-repo invocation provided.
Step 5 uses git add -A, violating CLAUDE.md safety rule to prefer specific file names and risking accidental inclusion of .env files in a tagged release commit.
Step 5 Task 6 uses git add -A for the final archive commit, risking accidental inclusion of .env files or credentials in a sealed archive push.
Cross-reference to safety.md uses relative filesystem path (.claude/rules/safety.md) that assumes a specific installation location.
Code Quality section scoping language ('after all work for a prompt is complete') differs from docs version ('run before pushing, regardless of mode').
Approval gates section lists seven points but Step 5 Task 5 (Stop Services) contains an eighth gate not listed.
Deep Mode description says 'all five categories' but never names them; the command explicitly names them.
Step 7 says max 4 options but the command's selection list only names four categories omitting Performance; users will see Performance findings but cannot select it independently.
Docs page omits 'Demo accounts or demo data' and 'Recommended restoration approach' items that appear in the step file scan list.
Docs page omits the report output format section entirely (eight sections defined in the step file), unlike every other archive docs page.
Consolidation decision table is missing a | --- | separator row, making it syntactically inconsistent with all other tables in the project.
Step 5 description says 'stops if origin still points to company repo' but the command actually guides reconfiguration interactively rather than halting.
Claim that plugin.json is 'the only version file' is not enforced and will become stale if a secondary version reference is added elsewhere.
Step 1 is labeled Branch check but lacks '## Step 1 —' prefix and uses --- separators inconsistently with how other commands structure their steps.
Scan exclusion list hard-codes PHP/Laravel paths (vendor/, storage/) with no equivalent exclusions for Python, Ruby, or Go projects.
Auto-Commit cross-reference wording differs between the shipped rule and the doc page, creating ambiguity about whether it is advisory or prescriptive.
Lint instruction 'Skip silently if not configured — never push with lint errors' is logically contradictory: skip implies no enforcement, never push implies enforcement.
Docs version of the lint instruction repeats the same logical ambiguity as rules/git.md without resolving it.
Document specs table for privacy-policy omits the Cookies section that the command requires as section 8 when cookies are detected.
'Archive' action value present in step file consolidation table but omitted from the corresponding docs page.
docker save command uses bare {image_name} placeholder but the filename uses {service} — inconsistent, undefined variable.
Opening instruction runs 'ls -la' unconditionally on invocation with no opt-out; every other command presents a check or confirmation before any action.

Project Config Check is referenced twice (Full and Gap sections) with the skip condition only described within the block itself, creating a hidden conditional agents must track across non-adjacent sections.
Branch structure list (feature/*, fix/*, chore/*, refactor/*) is an incomplete subset of Conventional Commits types; agents enforcing it would reject docs/, test/, perf/, ci/, build/ branches.
Restoration strategy matrix (lines 43–59) is duplicated almost verbatim in the docs page; two near-identical tables will inevitably drift.
Output format section (nearly half the file) restates rules already covered in the Rules section, inflating token cost and risking agents treating duplicated content as additive constraints.
Hard Safety Rules section repeats constraints already stated in Behavior Rules, adding length without adding safety guarantees.
Step 3 scans last 20 commits for Conventional Commits detection then separately runs git log for version calculation — two reads when one would serve both.
Deep Mode has no defined maximum chunk size or agent cap; total agent cost is unbounded on large monorepos.
Scan Targets list includes Laravel Sail — a Laravel-specific tool irrelevant to the majority of non-PHP projects.
Environment Branches section embeds git branch -r command inline as prose, coupling the rule to a specific detection mechanism.
Document specs table duplicates structural information already fully specified in the command's per-document sections, creating a second drift surface.
Tasks list restates content already fully specified in the main body sections (Consolidation, Required Files, Optional File) with no new information.
Install section has no verification or smoke-test step to confirm commands appear in the picker after /reload-plugins, leaving silent cache failures undetected.
Step 4 silently skips if no test framework detected — ship will tag, push, and create a GitHub Release with zero test validation.
No checkpoint requiring docker load validation on exported tarballs; a corrupt export will not be detected until actual recovery years later.
No minimum coverage threshold for 'representative requests'; an agent may verify a single health-check endpoint and mark the entire collection as tested.
Known Trap for stale plugin cache has no automated verification or scripted workaround — relies entirely on developer memory each release.
No review gate between generating documents and committing — writes and immediately commits legal documents without asking the user to review.
No post-write verification after copying rule files — command reports written/updated/skipped with no actual content or existence check.
Minimum Evidence Checklist says 'infer from CI/hooks/pipelines' but no self-check instruction tells agents how to verify the risk inventory is complete or current.
No verification step to confirm the remote branch was actually deleted (e.g. git ls-remote --heads origin) before proceeding.
No self-check instruction confirming every env file pattern (.env, .env.staging, .env.production, .env.*.local) was searched before declaring credentials risk complete.
Post-rewrite verification only checks last 10 commits via git log --oneline -10; rewrite failures in older history go undetected.
No self-check instruction for agents to verify mode detection (Solo vs GitHub Flow) resolved correctly before proceeding.
Stop conditions section does not list 'written tests fail' even though the command says it stops and informs before committing if tests fail.
Task 8 says 'verify every active document is reachable from README.md' with no concrete link-tracing check; verification is aspirational rather than testable.
References filter-branch as primary tool and notes it is deprecated since Git 2.36, but provides no concrete filter-repo invocation as the stated preferred alternative.
Hard-codes GitLab as the private archive remote destination; developers using GitHub, Gitea, or Forgejo will be confused by the GitLab-specific prompts.
plugin.json carries a version field but marketplace.json has no version field, making the marketplace registration version-unaware.
--generate-notes flag will produce empty/unhelpful release notes for repos that commit directly to main without PRs — the exact audience of this plugin.
Docker base image pinning guidance uses mutable tags (node:18.17.1-alpine) instead of immutable digests (@sha256:...), undermining the long-term recovery guarantee.
Scan Targets list references Docker, Lando, Laravel Sail but omits current tooling (Dev Containers, Podman, Fly.io), giving agents false confidence in infrastructure coverage.
docker save | gzip uses a shell pipe that may silently fail or produce an empty file in non-Unix Docker environments (Windows, remote Docker context).
Does not specify Postman Collection format version (v2.0 vs v2.1) or whether Bruno/Insomnia formats are acceptable; v2.0 is not importable by Bruno without conversion.
Jekyll remote theme pinned to just-the-docs@v0.10.0; may not be the latest stable release and could miss security or compatibility patches.
Commit message template uses {version} placeholder that is never defined or read anywhere in legal.md.
Cloud/infrastructure scan target lists specific vendors (AWS, DO, Cloudflare) but omits GCP, Azure, Supabase, Vercel, Neon.
Governing law clause says 'laws of the jurisdiction in which the author resides' without naming the jurisdiction, making it unenforceable.
Terms of Service governing law clause has the same unnamed-jurisdiction problem as the EULA.
